original_hypothesis.title: ETH locking is inconsistent with receive() reverting, causing forced-ETH balance divergence and potential accounting confusion
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Contract explicitly supports ETH locks via lock/lockOnBehalf when _tokenContract==address(0), yet receive() and fallback() revert. ETH can still be forcibly sent (selfdestruct/coinbase), creating a contract ETH balance that is not attributable to any user's lockedTokens. This can confuse operational accounting and incident response (users claim missing funds; auditors see surplus ETH).
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is operationally relevant on chains with selfdestruct semantics or forced ETH transfers, leading to real-world reconciliation issues. While not a direct theft vector, it complicates solvency monitoring and may mask shortfalls in other assets.
deployment_assumptions: The chain permits forced ETH transfers to contracts (e.g., via selfdestruct or protocol-level mechanisms)
Operational monitoring relies on contract.balance as a proxy for user-locked ETH
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: LockManager has receive()/fallback() reverting but exposes ETH lock path; no explicit ETH reconciliation or sweep function shown

proof_of_signal.pattern: receive() external payable { revert LockManagerRefuseETHError(); } while _lock allows _tokenContract==address(0) with msg.value == _quantity

preconditions: Some ETH is forcibly sent to LockManager outside lock/lockOnBehalf
Operators/accounting systems interpret contract ETH balance as user-backed collateral

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: ETH is both refused and accepted depending on call path, indicating potential monitoring and integration mismatches
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time forced ETH transfers occur

operational_questions: Do monitoring dashboards reconcile user-locked ETH vs address(this).balance?
Is there a procedure to handle unsolicited ETH (sweep, donate to treasury, etc.)?
Can forced ETH be used to make unlock failures appear as if the contract is solvent?
validation_requirements: Test forced ETH transfer to contract and confirm no state reflects it
Review operational runbooks for balance reconciliation

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Force-send ETH to LockManager (e.g., selfdestruct from another contract)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Observe ETH balance increases without corresponding lockedTokens entries
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Exploit confusion in monitoring/incident response (masking deficits elsewhere)
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None (attacker can do on-chain forced send if chain permits)

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Incorrect solvency reporting; delayed detection of deficits; user disputes
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low to medium (requires process fixes and monitoring improvements)

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Incoming ETH transfers with no corresponding Locked events
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Contract ETH balance > sum of user-locked ETH quantities
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Small sporadic ETH dusting to LockManager

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
